flashcards.front: What is the relationship between user-level threads and kernel-level threads in multithreading?
flashcards.back: User-level threads are created and managed entirely in user space without kernel involvement, while kernel-level threads are managed by the operating system kernel. The multithreading model defines how these two types of threads are mapped to each other.

flashcards.front: Describe the Many-to-One Model in multithreading.
flashcards.back: In the Many-to-One model, multiple user threads are mapped to a single kernel thread. Thread management is done in user space by the thread library. If a user thread blocks, all associated threads block because only one kernel thread exists.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages and disadvantages of the Many-to-One model?
flashcards.back: Advantages: High efficiency (no kernel intervention), good portability across OS. Disadvantages: Cannot utilize multiprocessors, if one user thread blocks (system call), all threads block.

flashcards.front: Explain the One-to-One Model in multithreading.
flashcards.back: In the One-to-One model, each user thread is mapped to a separate kernel thread. This provides better concurrency allowing multiple threads to run in parallel on multiprocessors. Disadvantage is overhead of creating kernel threads.
The Many-to-Many model multiplexes many user threads to an equal or lesser number of kernel threads. User threads can be scheduled to run on available kernel threads, achieving true concurrency.

flashcards.front: How does the Two-level Model differ from the Many-to-Many model?
flashcards.back: The Two-level Model is a variation where some user threads are bound to a dedicated kernel thread (one-to-one) while other user threads are multiplexed to fewer kernel threads (many-to-many). This provides both parallelism and flexibility.

flashcards.front: What is a thread library? Give examples.
flashcards.back: A thread library provides API for creating and managing threads. Examples include: POSIX Pthreads (POSIX standard), Windows Threads (Win32 API), Java Threads (JVM).
Thread scheduling determines the order in which threads are executed on CPU. It involves deciding which thread runs next when the current thread yields, blocks, or terminates.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive thread scheduling?
flashcards.back: In preemptive scheduling, the OS can forcibly suspend a running thread to allow another thread to execute. In non-preemptive scheduling, a thread runs until it voluntarily yields or blocks.

flashcards.front: What are the scheduling criteria for multithreading?
flashcards.back: Key criteria include: CPU Utilization (keep CPU busy), Throughput (processes completed per unit time), Turnaround Time (submission to completion), Waiting Time (time in ready queue), Response Time (first response).
First-Come-First-Served (FCFS) schedules threads in the order they arrive in the ready queue. Non-preemptive. Simple but can lead to convoy effect where short processes wait for long ones.
Shortest Job First schedules the thread with the smallest burst time next. Can be preemptive (Shortest Remaining Time First) or non-preemptive. Optimal for minimizing average waiting time.
Round Robin assigns a fixed time quantum to each thread in the ready queue. Threads are executed in FCFS order but each gets only quantum time. Good for time-sharing systems.
Priority scheduling executes threads based on priority levels. Higher priority threads run first. Can suffer from starvation of low-priority threads (solution: aging - gradually increase priority).

Multiprocessor scheduling assigns threads to multiple processors. Approaches include: load balancing, processor affinity (keep thread on same processor for cache efficiency), and symmetric vs asymmetric multiprocessing.
